description: Candidate cities for nightly LVT modeling, ranked by a priori political viability. Factors: clarity of state legal pathway, current governance lean, housing crisis severity, presence of local LVT advocacy, and demonstrated policy momentum. Update rank/score as models ship and political conditions change.

legal_pathway.clear: State already has enabling legislation for split-rate or differential land taxation (PA, VA)
legal_pathway.moderate: Dem-controlled state legislature where enabling bill is plausible in 1-2 sessions (MN, VT, RI, OR)
legal_pathway.challenging: Republican-controlled legislature or constitutional barrier (most other states)

scoring_notes.score: 0-100 composite of legal pathway (40%), governance lean (25%), housing crisis severity (20%), LVT advocacy presence (15%)

candidates.rationale: Strongest US precedent: Pittsburgh had a graded land tax from 1913 to 2001 — when it was abolished during an assessment crisis, not by policy choice. Progressive mayor Ed Gainey (2022–), progressive council majority. Strong academic champions at Carnegie Mellon and Pitt; Lincoln Institute of Land Policy connections. Active local advocacy to restore the graded tax. PA enabling legislation (Act 188 of 1984) is already on the books. Abolition-of-the-graded-tax is a documented case study making this uniquely tractable politically.

Pennsylvania's state capital under severe fiscal stress. Progressive Democratic administration. Long history of blight and vacancy. PA enabling law is already in place. Legislators are unusually aware of LVT because it comes up in state budget debates. A model here would be directly in front of the lawmakers who could expand enabling legislation statewide.

Virginia Code §58.1-3220 enables jurisdictions to adopt a differential land tax rate. Arlington is one of the most progressive jurisdictions in the United States — all-Democratic county board, very high renter share, ongoing Missing Middle Housing fights. DC Metro housing crisis is severe. The connection between upzoning advocacy (which Arlington already did) and LVT is natural and politically legible here.

Extremely progressive city government (DFL supermajority), first major US city to end single-family zoning (Minneapolis 2040 Plan). Strong housing advocacy coalition. St. Paul already modeled — Minneapolis as the larger Twin City creates a powerful comparative pair. MN DFL controls the legislature and has shown interest in property tax reform. Active land economics discussion in local policy circles.

Virginia enabling law (§58.1-3220). Alexandria is a progressive independent city in the DC Metro area with a housing affordability crisis. Smaller and more tractable than Arlington for an initial VA test case. Democratic city council. High land values driven by DC proximity make the split-rate impact large and politically legible.

One of the worst housing crises in the US. New strong-mayor charter took effect 2025. Oregon has a Dem-controlled legislature and has shown interest in housing reform; enabling legislation is politically plausible. Strong housing advocacy organizations (Meyer Memorial Trust, Upstream Public Health). The housing crisis narrative makes LVT reform politically legible to voters.

Land speculation and vacancy are the defining economic issues of Detroit — LVT is directly responsive. Progressive city government. Large academic and policy community (University of Michigan, Wayne State) with demonstrated interest in land economics. Michigan would need enabling legislation but Dem-controlled legislature as of recent sessions. The Detroit Land Bank Authority already operates in the LVT conceptual space.

Yale University creates massive distortions in the New Haven property market (PILOT payments, huge exempt land). Yale economists have studied LVT; Yale Law housing clinic is active. Progressive mayor, Democratic council. CT would need enabling legislation but has a Dem trifecta. High political salience of Yale's tax-exempt status makes LVT a natural complement to existing policy debates.

Virginia enabling law (§58.1-3220). Progressive city government. Significant gentrification pressure in Church Hill, Scott's Addition, Manchester. Richmond is larger and more complex than Charlottesville (already modeled), providing a richer VA data point and a model that speaks to a larger electorate.

Pennsylvania's third-largest city. Clear PA enabling law. Rising housing costs with a majority-Latino population facing displacement pressure. Progressive Democratic administration. A model here would show LVT's equity implications in a majority-minority mid-size city — a politically important framing for the broader PA case.

Explosive growth with one of the worst housing cost trajectories in the South. Progressive mayor Freddie O'Connell (2023–). Conservative TN legislature is the main barrier, but Nashville's local political will is strong. The housing crisis is severe enough that even conservative TN legislators are under pressure. A model would arm Nashville advocates with concrete numbers when they approach the state legislature.

Post-industrial city with significant vacant land, land speculation in reviving neighborhoods (Elmwood Village, Allentown), and a progressive base. New York state has Dem trifecta and has considered property tax reform repeatedly. UB (SUNY Buffalo) has academic interest in urban economics. Proximity to Niagara Falls and Toronto creates cross-border land value dynamics worth studying.

One of the fastest-growing metros in the US with severe affordability collapse. Unique coalition potential: libertarian-leaning Idaho electorate + housing crisis = some free-market support for LVT as an alternative to restrictive zoning. State legislature is Republican-controlled but the housing crisis has produced unusual cross-partisan pressure. A model would arm Boise advocates for a long-horizon state legislative push.

Extremely progressive university city (Mayor Satya Rhodes-Conway). Significant housing shortage driven by UW-Madison enrollment. WI would need enabling legislation, but Madison has disproportionate state political influence. Strong academic community (UW-Madison economics) with land economics interest. High political salience of housing costs for students and families creates favorable conditions.

Surprising progressive enclave in conservative south-central PA. Progressive Democratic mayor (Danene Sorace). Growing gentrification and housing cost pressure driven by Philadelphia spillover. PA enabling law is already in place. A successful model here would demonstrate LVT is viable even in politically mixed regions of PA, strengthening the statewide case.

Progressive Democratic mayor Cavalier Johnson. Significant housing issues and displacement in historically Black neighborhoods (Bronzeville, Harambee). WI enabling legislation needed but Milwaukee has state political influence. The racial equity framing of LVT is politically powerful here.

Clear PA enabling law. Post-industrial city with significant blight and vacancy challenges. Mayor Joe Schember (D). Affordable housing market overall, but the blight/vacancy issue makes LVT especially relevant — land value taxation is a proven tool for incentivizing vacant land development. The PA precedent from Erie would help the statewide case.

Progressive Mayor Erin Mendenhall in a blue island in a very red state. 4th worst housing affordability by some metrics. Unusual bipartisan potential: LDS Church historical interest in stewardship and land use + libertarian UT voters + housing crisis pressure. Conservative state legislature is the main barrier, but the housing emergency has been producing unusual political pressure.

One of Missouri's most progressive cities. Rapid growth, significant gentrification concerns, strong housing advocacy community. MO would need enabling legislation and the legislature is Republican-controlled. However Kansas City's political organization is strong enough to make a long-horizon state-level push plausible, and a model arms that effort.

Progressive mayor Andre Dickens (2022–), Democratic city council. Major housing crisis, massive displacement in Vine City, Mechanicsville, and West End. GA legislature is Republican-controlled, but Atlanta's political organization and advocacy community are strong. A model for Atlanta would be politically useful given Atlanta's national profile.

Connecticut's capital city is severely fiscally stressed — LVT could be framed as a fiscal stabilization tool as much as a housing reform. Progressive Democratic city. CT has a Dem trifecta and has repeatedly studied property tax reform. High political salience of Hartford's fiscal crisis creates unusual openness to structural solutions.

Bernie Sanders' former city. Extremely progressive. Severe housing shortage driven by UVM + tourism. VT has a Dem-controlled legislature and is a small state where legislative change is more achievable. Strong progressive political infrastructure. A Burlington model would have significant national symbolic value.

Urban decline and land speculation are central to St. Louis's policy debates — LVT is directly responsive. Progressive Democratic city government. Land value issues are well-documented academically (Washington University economists). The city-county split creates interesting modeling challenges. MO enabling legislation is a barrier but St. Louis has strong national policy network connections.

PA enabling law already in place. Reading has one of the highest poverty rates among US cities of its size, with a high property tax burden relative to income. A split-rate model would show large equity benefits for low-income homeowners. Democratic city. The equity framing is compelling: LVT shifts burden from improvements (homes) to land (often held by speculators).

Virginia enabling law (§58.1-3220). Moderate-to-progressive city government. Growing housing pressure in the Roanoke Valley. Smaller and more moderate than Richmond but the legal pathway is identical. A Roanoke model would expand the VA portfolio and demonstrate LVT viability in smaller VA cities.

Unique post-Katrina land patterns with significant vacancy, blight, and speculation. Progressive mayor LaToya Cantrell. Housing affordability crisis driven by tourism and short-term rentals. LA legislature is Republican-controlled, but New Orleans's political organization is strong. The unique land value dynamics post-Katrina make this an academically interesting case study.

Progressive Democratic mayor Brett Smiley (2023–). Growing housing cost pressure from Boston-Providence corridor. Rhode Island is a small state with a Dem-controlled legislature where enabling legislation is achievable. Brown University economists and policy school provide academic backing. The small-state dynamic means a single well-networked champion can move legislation.

Progressive Democratic mayor Regina Romero. Growing housing affordability issues. AZ legislature is Republican-controlled and has been actively preempting local housing policy. Near-term legislative pathway is limited, but Tucson is a useful model for the Sun Belt growth context and longer-horizon advocacy.

Fastest-growing Midwestern city with significant housing pressure and displacement. Progressive Democratic mayor. OH would need enabling legislation and the legislature is Republican-controlled. However Columbus has disproportionate state influence and a strong civic and academic community (Ohio State). Long-horizon case.

Post-industrial Ohio city with a new progressive mayor (Shammas Chitty, elected 2024). Interesting comparison to Cleveland (already modeled). Land value issues common to post-industrial Midwest. OH enabling legislation is a barrier but the post-industrial context makes LVT theoretically compelling, and an Akron model pairs well with the existing Cleveland model.
